catalyst.source: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 in seawater into chemical fuel, methanol (CH 3 OH), was achieved over CuC-co-doped TiO 2 nanoparticles under UV and natural sunlight.
catalyst.context: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 in seawater into chemical fuel, methanol (CH 3 OH), was achieved over CuC-co-doped TiO 2 nanoparticles under UV and natural sunlight.Photocatalysts with different Cu loadings (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, and 7 wt) were synthesized by the sol-gel method and were characterized by XRD, SEM, UV-Vis, FTIR, and XPS.Co-doping with C and Cu into TiO 2 remarkably promoted the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH.This improvement was attributed to lowering of bandgap energy, specific catalytic effect of Cu for CH 3 OH formation, and the minimization of photo-generated carrier recombination.Co-doped TiO 2 with 3.0 wt Cu was found to be the most active catalyst, giving a maximum methanol yield rate of 577 mol g-cat -1 h -1 under illumination of UV light, which is 5.3-fold higher than the production rate over C-TiO 2 and 7.4 times the amount produced using Degussa P25 TiO 2 .Under natural sunlight, the maximum rate of the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH using 3.0 wt CuC-TiO 2 was found to be 188 mol g-cat -1 h -1 , which is 2.24 times higher than that of C-TiO 2 , whereas, no CH 3 OH was observed for P25.Keywords CO 2 photoreduction .CuC-co-doped TiO 2 .Methanol .Copper .Seawater

catalyst.source: Co-doped TiO 2 with 3.0 wt Cu was found to be the most active catalyst, giving a maximum methanol yield rate of 577 mol g-cat -1 h -1 under illumination of UV light, which is 5.3-fold higher than the production rate over C-TiO 2 and 7.4 times the amount produced using Degussa P25 TiO 2 .

catalyst.source: The synthesis of unmodified titanium dioxide (n-TiO 2 ) was achieved by hydrolysis followed by oxidation of titanium trichloride in an aqueous medium.
catalyst.context: The synthesis of unmodified titanium dioxide (n-TiO 2 ) was achieved by hydrolysis followed by oxidation of titanium trichloride in an aqueous medium.Titanium trichloride (TiCl 3 12.5 in hydrochloric acid (6-12, Sigma-Aldrich)) was added dropwise with continuous stirring into double distilled water.The strength of titanium was ajusted to 0.15 M. Subsequently, the pH of the resultant violet solution was attuned between 2.5 and 4.0 using sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution.The resultant solution was filtered and cleaned several times with deionized water, then heated in an oven at 80 C for 24 h, and finally calcined at 300 C for 2 h. The sol-gel method was selected for the synthesis of CuC-TiO 2 , by dropwise addition of 30 ml of titanium (IV) isopropoxide to 30 ml of absolute ethanol with the aid of constant sonication.Then, 15 ml of glucose (0.015 M) and different weight percentages of Cu(II) were added to the prepared mixture.The pH of the resultant gel was adjusted to 3 using 50 HNO 3 , and the sonication was continued for further 2 h.The formed gel was reserved around 1 day at room temperature and then dried at 80 C for 12 h.The synthesized powder was transferred into a crucible and kept it in a muffle furnace at 500 C for 2 h.The CuC-TiO 2 was prepared by following the same procedure as C-TiO 2 , except the addition of Cu solution.TiO 2 P25 (Degussa, Japan) was used, without any additional modification, as a standard reference catalyst for comparison. A Shimadzu X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6000) equipped with Cu K radiation operating at 30 mA and 40 kV was used to determine the crystallinity of the catalysts.X-ray data were collected over the 2 range of 20-70at a scan rate of 1.0min -1 .The optical properties of the photocatalysts were evaluated by a Shimadzu, PharmaSpec UV-1700 Spectrophotometer.A JSM-7600F, JEOL (USA) Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) attached with Energy-Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopic unit (EDS, X-Max 50 mm 2 , Oxford Instruments) was used to investigate the surface morphology and the elemental composition of the photocatalysts.Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, UK) was used to study the chemical structure and functional groups present in the photocatalysts.The samples were scanned within the wave range of 400-4000 cm -1 .X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on SPECS surface analysis systems operating at a base pressure of 4 10 -10 mbar.A dual-anode non-monochromatic Mg-K (1253.6 eV) X-ray source was used to irradiate the sample surface with 13.5 kV and 150 W of X-ray power. The photocatalytic reduction experiments were carried out in a homemade batch annular reactor.The reactor was made up of Pyrex glass and was coupled to the CO 2 cylinder with a firmly closed tube.Liquid samples were taken out at regular interval of time using a syringe, which is linked to the reactor through an extra opening.The light source from the combination of 254 and 365 nm UV lamps (Sankyo Denki, Germicidal Lamp, G15 T8, Japan) was irradiated to all the sides of the reactor.Before going to irradiation, a certified highpurity super-critical fluid-grade carbon dioxide was used with a constant flow through the stirred suspension for at least 1 h to purge the air and to saturate the solution.The reactor was designed accordingly to maintain the constant CO 2 pressure.In order to avoid the sedimentation of the catalyst, the photocatalytic reaction was carried out under constant stirring with the aid of a magnetic stirrer.The reaction temperature and pH were monitored throughout the experiment.The desired catalyst loading was suspended in seawater sample, collected from coastal sites in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, and then illuminated with UV and natural sunlight.The chemical characteristics and the concentrations of the major ions of the studied seawater samples are listed in Table S1 (Supplementary material).Samples were withdrawn at regular interval of time, and the level of CO 2 was measured by both GC and titrimetric method.The produced CH 3 OH was measured by the spectrophotometric method (Anpo et al. 1997;Zhan et al. 2010).Different blank tests were performed in order to show the products that are formed on the influence of photocatalyst and light energy.

catalyst.source: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 and CH 3 OH yield were successfully achieved in seawater using CuC-TiO 2 nanoparticles under both UV and natural sunlight.
catalyst.context: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 and CH 3 OH yield were successfully achieved in seawater using CuC-TiO 2 nanoparticles under both UV and natural sunlight.Under the current experimental condition, the optimum loading was achieved by the doping of C-TiO 2 by 3 wt of Cu.The highest CH 3 OH yield was obtained over 1 g l -1 of 3 wt CuC-TiO 2 .The experimental results revealed that the production of CH 3 OH using CuC-codoped-TiO 2 is much higher than that of P25 and C-TiO 2 , due to the effective trapping of the charge carriers and the inhibition of their recombination rate by Cu doping, as well as the bandgap narrowing, as a result of C doping. Fig. 1 XRD patterns of the photocatalysts Fig. 2 Transformed Kubelka-Munk function of the photocatalysts Fig. 5 Effect of Cu loading amount on CH 3 OH yield under UV irradiation Fig. 7 Time dependence on CO 2 reduction (a) and CH 3 OH yield (b) by using P25, C-TiO 2 , and various CuC-TiO 2 catalysts under UV light Fig. 8 Time dependence on CO 2 reduction (a) and CH 3 OH yield (b) by using P25, C-TiO 2 , and 3 wt CuC-TiO 2 under real sunlight Elemental analysis of P25, CTiO 2 , and 3 wt CuC- Acknowledgments The author, Yasar N Kavil, is grateful to the Deanship of Graduate Studies, King Abdulaziz University, for providing a Ph.D. fellowship.

co-catalyst.source: Co-doping with C and Cu into TiO 2 remarkably promoted the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH.
co-catalyst.context: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 in seawater into chemical fuel, methanol (CH 3 OH), was achieved over CuC-co-doped TiO 2 nanoparticles under UV and natural sunlight.Photocatalysts with different Cu loadings (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, and 7 wt) were synthesized by the sol-gel method and were characterized by XRD, SEM, UV-Vis, FTIR, and XPS.Co-doping with C and Cu into TiO 2 remarkably promoted the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH.This improvement was attributed to lowering of bandgap energy, specific catalytic effect of Cu for CH 3 OH formation, and the minimization of photo-generated carrier recombination.Co-doped TiO 2 with 3.0 wt Cu was found to be the most active catalyst, giving a maximum methanol yield rate of 577 mol g-cat -1 h -1 under illumination of UV light, which is 5.3-fold higher than the production rate over C-TiO 2 and 7.4 times the amount produced using Degussa P25 TiO 2 .Under natural sunlight, the maximum rate of the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH using 3.0 wt CuC-TiO 2 was found to be 188 mol g-cat -1 h -1 , which is 2.24 times higher than that of C-TiO 2 , whereas, no CH 3 OH was observed for P25.Keywords CO 2 photoreduction .CuC-co-doped TiO 2 .Methanol .Copper .Seawater

co-catalyst.source: Under the current experimental condition, the optimum loading was achieved by the doping of C-TiO 2 by 3 wt of Cu.
co-catalyst.context: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 and CH 3 OH yield were successfully achieved in seawater using CuC-TiO 2 nanoparticles under both UV and natural sunlight.Under the current experimental condition, the optimum loading was achieved by the doping of C-TiO 2 by 3 wt of Cu.The highest CH 3 OH yield was obtained over 1 g l -1 of 3 wt CuC-TiO 2 .The experimental results revealed that the production of CH 3 OH using CuC-codoped-TiO 2 is much higher than that of P25 and C-TiO 2 , due to the effective trapping of the charge carriers and the inhibition of their recombination rate by Cu doping, as well as the bandgap narrowing, as a result of C doping. light source.context: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 in seawater into chemical fuel, methanol (CH 3 OH), was achieved over CuC-co-doped TiO 2 nanoparticles under UV and natural sunlight.Photocatalysts with different Cu loadings (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, and 7 wt) were synthesized by the sol-gel method and were characterized by XRD, SEM, UV-Vis, FTIR, and XPS.Co-doping with C and Cu into TiO 2 remarkably promoted the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH.This improvement was attributed to lowering of bandgap energy, specific catalytic effect of Cu for CH 3 OH formation, and the minimization of photo-generated carrier recombination.Co-doped TiO 2 with 3.0 wt Cu was found to be the most active catalyst, giving a maximum methanol yield rate of 577 mol g-cat -1 h -1 under illumination of UV light, which is 5.3-fold higher than the production rate over C-TiO 2 and 7.4 times the amount produced using Degussa P25 TiO 2 .Under natural sunlight, the maximum rate of the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH using 3.0 wt CuC-TiO 2 was found to be 188 mol g-cat -1 h -1 , which is 2.24 times higher than that of C-TiO 2 , whereas, no CH 3 OH was observed for P25.Keywords CO 2 photoreduction .CuC-co-doped TiO 2 .Methanol .Copper .Seawater

light source.source: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 in seawater into chemical fuel, methanol (CH 3 OH), was achieved over CuC-co-doped TiO 2 nanoparticles under UV and natural sunlight.
light source.context: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 in seawater into chemical fuel, methanol (CH 3 OH), was achieved over CuC-co-doped TiO 2 nanoparticles under UV and natural sunlight.Photocatalysts with different Cu loadings (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, and 7 wt) were synthesized by the sol-gel method and were characterized by XRD, SEM, UV-Vis, FTIR, and XPS.Co-doping with C and Cu into TiO 2 remarkably promoted the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH.This improvement was attributed to lowering of bandgap energy, specific catalytic effect of Cu for CH 3 OH formation, and the minimization of photo-generated carrier recombination.Co-doped TiO 2 with 3.0 wt Cu was found to be the most active catalyst, giving a maximum methanol yield rate of 577 mol g-cat -1 h -1 under illumination of UV light, which is 5.3-fold higher than the production rate over C-TiO 2 and 7.4 times the amount produced using Degussa P25 TiO 2 .Under natural sunlight, the maximum rate of the photocatalytic production of CH 3 OH using 3.0 wt CuC-TiO 2 was found to be 188 mol g-cat -1 h -1 , which is 2.24 times higher than that of C-TiO 2 , whereas, no CH 3 OH was observed for P25.Keywords CO 2 photoreduction .CuC-co-doped TiO 2 .Methanol .Copper .Seawater

light source.source: The light source from the combination of 254 and 365 nm UV lamps (Sankyo Denki, Germicidal Lamp, G15 T8, Japan) was irradiated to all the sides of the reactor.
light source.context: The synthesis of unmodified titanium dioxide (n-TiO 2 ) was achieved by hydrolysis followed by oxidation of titanium trichloride in an aqueous medium.Titanium trichloride (TiCl 3 12.5 in hydrochloric acid (6-12, Sigma-Aldrich)) was added dropwise with continuous stirring into double distilled water.The strength of titanium was ajusted to 0.15 M. Subsequently, the pH of the resultant violet solution was attuned between 2.5 and 4.0 using sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution.The resultant solution was filtered and cleaned several times with deionized water, then heated in an oven at 80 C for 24 h, and finally calcined at 300 C for 2 h. The sol-gel method was selected for the synthesis of CuC-TiO 2 , by dropwise addition of 30 ml of titanium (IV) isopropoxide to 30 ml of absolute ethanol with the aid of constant sonication.Then, 15 ml of glucose (0.015 M) and different weight percentages of Cu(II) were added to the prepared mixture.The pH of the resultant gel was adjusted to 3 using 50 HNO 3 , and the sonication was continued for further 2 h.The formed gel was reserved around 1 day at room temperature and then dried at 80 C for 12 h.The synthesized powder was transferred into a crucible and kept it in a muffle furnace at 500 C for 2 h.The CuC-TiO 2 was prepared by following the same procedure as C-TiO 2 , except the addition of Cu solution.TiO 2 P25 (Degussa, Japan) was used, without any additional modification, as a standard reference catalyst for comparison. A Shimadzu X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6000) equipped with Cu K radiation operating at 30 mA and 40 kV was used to determine the crystallinity of the catalysts.X-ray data were collected over the 2 range of 20-70at a scan rate of 1.0min -1 .The optical properties of the photocatalysts were evaluated by a Shimadzu, PharmaSpec UV-1700 Spectrophotometer.A JSM-7600F, JEOL (USA) Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) attached with Energy-Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopic unit (EDS, X-Max 50 mm 2 , Oxford Instruments) was used to investigate the surface morphology and the elemental composition of the photocatalysts.Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, UK) was used to study the chemical structure and functional groups present in the photocatalysts.The samples were scanned within the wave range of 400-4000 cm -1 .X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on SPECS surface analysis systems operating at a base pressure of 4 10 -10 mbar.A dual-anode non-monochromatic Mg-K (1253.6 eV) X-ray source was used to irradiate the sample surface with 13.5 kV and 150 W of X-ray power. The photocatalytic reduction experiments were carried out in a homemade batch annular reactor.The reactor was made up of Pyrex glass and was coupled to the CO 2 cylinder with a firmly closed tube.Liquid samples were taken out at regular interval of time using a syringe, which is linked to the reactor through an extra opening.The light source from the combination of 254 and 365 nm UV lamps (Sankyo Denki, Germicidal Lamp, G15 T8, Japan) was irradiated to all the sides of the reactor.Before going to irradiation, a certified highpurity super-critical fluid-grade carbon dioxide was used with a constant flow through the stirred suspension for at least 1 h to purge the air and to saturate the solution.The reactor was designed accordingly to maintain the constant CO 2 pressure.In order to avoid the sedimentation of the catalyst, the photocatalytic reaction was carried out under constant stirring with the aid of a magnetic stirrer.The reaction temperature and pH were monitored throughout the experiment.The desired catalyst loading was suspended in seawater sample, collected from coastal sites in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, and then illuminated with UV and natural sunlight.The chemical characteristics and the concentrations of the major ions of the studied seawater samples are listed in Table S1 (Supplementary material).Samples were withdrawn at regular interval of time, and the level of CO 2 was measured by both GC and titrimetric method.The produced CH 3 OH was measured by the spectrophotometric method (Anpo et al. 1997;Zhan et al. 2010).Different blank tests were performed in order to show the products that are formed on the influence of photocatalyst and light energy.

light source.source: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 and CH 3 OH yield were successfully achieved in seawater using CuC-TiO 2 nanoparticles under both UV and natural sunlight.
light source.context: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 and CH 3 OH yield were successfully achieved in seawater using CuC-TiO 2 nanoparticles under both UV and natural sunlight.Under the current experimental condition, the optimum loading was achieved by the doping of C-TiO 2 by 3 wt of Cu.The highest CH 3 OH yield was obtained over 1 g l -1 of 3 wt CuC-TiO 2 .The experimental results revealed that the production of CH 3 OH using CuC-codoped-TiO 2 is much higher than that of P25 and C-TiO 2 , due to the effective trapping of the charge carriers and the inhibition of their recombination rate by Cu doping, as well as the bandgap narrowing, as a result of C doping. Fig. 1 XRD patterns of the photocatalysts Fig. 2 Transformed Kubelka-Munk function of the photocatalysts Fig. 5 Effect of Cu loading amount on CH 3 OH yield under UV irradiation Fig. 7 Time dependence on CO 2 reduction (a) and CH 3 OH yield (b) by using P25, C-TiO 2 , and various CuC-TiO 2 catalysts under UV light Fig. 8 Time dependence on CO 2 reduction (a) and CH 3 OH yield (b) by using P25, C-TiO 2 , and 3 wt CuC-TiO 2 under real sunlight Elemental analysis of P25, CTiO 2 , and 3 wt CuC- Acknowledgments The author, Yasar N Kavil, is grateful to the Deanship of Graduate Studies, King Abdulaziz University, for providing a Ph.D. fellowship.
